docs.title: Pololu Romi — Term Project
docs.text:
Course: ME 405-Mechatronics

Team / Authors: Oliver Lenoir, Lucas Nathan, Vandan Patel

Olenoir@calpoly.edu, Lrnathan@calpoly.edu, Vpatel43@calpoly.edu

Project Summary: building and programming a Pololu Romi robot. This site documents design goals, hardware used, drivers implemented (motors, encoders, line sensor, IMU), test results, and full source code.

docs.title: Course Summary
docs.text:
Over the course of this class, our lab group gradually built up the software and hardware needed to run and control our Romi robot. At the beginning of the quarter, we focused on the basics of programming the Nucleo board using MicroPython. These early labs were mainly about learning how the microcontroller interacts with hardware such as motors, encoders, and timers. This gave us a foundation for understanding how embedded software controls real physical systems.

Once we were comfortable working with the hardware, we started organizing our code using cooperative multitasking. Instead of writing one large program, we split our system into multiple tasks that run alongside each other and share information when needed. We also implemented finite state machines to control how different parts of the program behave. This structure made our code easier to expand as we added more features throughout the quarter.

After establishing this structure, we began integrating sensors into the robot. Our group wired and programmed the line sensor and the bump sensors so the robot could detect its environment. The bump sensors were connected through GPIO pins and allow the robot to detect when it physically contacts an obstacle. The line sensor array was wired to read reflectance values from the ground so the robot can determine its position relative to a line. We then wrote code to read and interpret these signals inside our task system so the robot could react to them in real time.

Once the robot could sense its surroundings, we worked on improving its motion using closed loop motor control. Using feedback from the wheel encoders, we implemented a controller that regulates motor speed and allows the robot to reach and maintain a desired velocity. This step introduced us to control system concepts and required tuning the controller gains to achieve a stable and responsive step response.

Later in the project we incorporated the IMU to perform state estimation. The IMU provides measurements of acceleration and angular velocity, which can be used to estimate the robot’s orientation and movement over time. We added a task that reads the IMU data sequentially and processes it so the robot can estimate its heading and rotational motion while it drives. This information works alongside the encoder and line sensor data to give a better overall picture of the robot’s state.

To help analyze the performance of our control system, we also implemented a serial user interface and automated data collection system. Through the serial terminal we can enter controller gains, choose setpoints, and trigger step response tests. A Python script running on a computer communicates with the microcontroller, collects the output data, and automatically generates plots of the results for analysis.

The final term project ties all of these pieces together. Throughout the quarter we developed drivers, sensor interfaces, control algorithms, and a structured multitasking program. In the final project we combine the motors, encoders, IMU, line sensors, bump sensors, and all of the software we wrote to complete the course challenge. The result is a fully integrated robotic system that builds directly on each stage of the work we completed earlier in the class.

docs.title: main.py
docs.text: The main entry point of the program. This file initializes all hardware interfaces, creates shared variables, instantiates tasks, and starts the cooperative scheduler that runs the robot's control system.

docs.title: cotask.py
docs.text: Implements the cooperative multitasking scheduler used in the project. It manages task priorities, timing, and execution so multiple control tasks can run concurrently without blocking each other.

docs.title: task_share.py
docs.text: Provides the Share and Queue classes used for communication between tasks. These shared data structures allow tasks to safely exchange information such as sensor readings, velocity commands, and control flags.

docs.title: task_user.py
docs.text: Implements the user interface task that communicates with the robot through the serial terminal. It processes commands such as calibration and starting the robot while updating shared variables used by other tasks.

docs.title: task_motor.py
docs.text: Handles closed-loop control of each motor using encoder feedback. This task reads encoder data, applies the PI control algorithm, and sets the PWM duty cycle required to achieve the desired wheel velocity.

docs.title: motor_driver.py
docs.text: A hardware abstraction class for controlling the Romi motors. It converts control signals from the motor task into PWM outputs and direction signals for the motor driver circuitry.

docs.title: encoder.py
docs.text: Implements an interface for reading quadrature encoder signals from the wheel motors. It converts raw timer counts into position and velocity measurements used for motor control and state estimation.

docs.title: task_line.py
docs.text: Implements the line-following behavior using data from the line sensor array. It calculates the line position error and adjusts the left and right wheel speeds using a PI control strategy to keep the robot centered on the line.

docs.title: line_sensor.py
docs.text: Provides a driver for the QTR-8A reflective infrared sensor array used for line detection. It reads analog sensor values and computes a centroid that represents the position of the line relative to the robot.

docs.title: task_recover.py
docs.text: Controls the robot’s higher-level navigation and recovery behaviors. It manages sequences such as line loss recovery, heading changes, bump sensor responses, and special navigation states.

docs.title: heading_nav.py
docs.text: Implements heading-based navigation utilities using IMU data. It allows the robot to maintain or turn to specific headings while driving.

docs.title: IMU_driver.py
docs.text: Low-level driver for communicating with the BNO055 inertial measurement unit. It reads orientation and angular velocity data used for navigation and heading control.

docs.title: IMU_task.py
docs.text: A task responsible for continuously reading the IMU and updating shared variables with orientation and angular velocity measurements.

docs.title: task_estimator.py
docs.text: Implements the robot state estimator that tracks position and orientation using encoder and IMU data. It provides estimated state variables used by navigation and control tasks.

docs.title: Matrices.py
docs.text: Contains the state-space matrices used by the estimator model. These matrices define the mathematical relationship between system inputs, states, and outputs.

docs.title: Matrix calculations.py
docs.text: A helper script used to compute or verify the matrices used by the estimator. This file is typically used offline for development and testing rather than running on the robot.
